1960s.title: Oil revenue and modernization pressures
1960s.body: Iran's 1960s economy mixed oil revenue, state-led modernization, and urban wage growth, with consumer prices shaped by rapid structural change.
1960s.event: White Revolution era and oil-backed development
1960s.driver: Oil revenue, wages, imported goods, urban demand
1960s.outcome: The rial's purchasing power starts before revolution, war, and sanctions reshaped the economy.
1960s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1970s.title: Oil boom, revolution, and price instability
1970s.body: The oil boom increased state spending, while the 1979 revolution disrupted institutions, trade, and expectations around prices.
1970s.driver: Oil revenue, public spending, political disruption
1970s.outcome: The decade bridges oil-financed expansion and revolutionary uncertainty.

1980s.title: War economy and rationing
1980s.body: The Iran-Iraq War brought rationing, fiscal pressure, and supply disruption, changing how households experienced prices even when controls were in place.
1980s.outcome: Official CPI captures prices, but wartime scarcity also affected lived purchasing power.
1980s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1990s.title: Reconstruction and exchange-rate pressure
1990s.body: Postwar reconstruction, multiple exchange rates, and fiscal adjustment shaped inflation as Iran rebuilt after the war.
1990s.outcome: Purchasing power weakened as rebuilding costs and monetary pressures accumulated.

2000s.title: Oil income, subsidies, and sanctions risk
2000s.body: Oil revenue supported spending, while subsidy policy, sanctions risk, and currency management shaped the cost of imported and essential goods.
2000s.event: Oil revenue cycle and rising sanctions pressure
2000s.driver: Oil prices, subsidies, exchange rates, imports
2000s.outcome: The rial's purchasing power remained exposed to policy and external constraints.
2000s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

2010s.title: Sanctions and currency depreciation
2010s.body: International sanctions, exchange-rate depreciation, and subsidy reforms made inflation a central household and policy issue.
2010s.event: Sanctions escalation, JCPOA period, and renewed sanctions
2010s.driver: Currency depreciation, import costs, subsidy reform
2010s.outcome: Purchasing power losses accelerated when exchange-rate pressure reached consumer prices.

2020s.title: High inflation under sanctions pressure
2020s.body: Currency stress, sanctions, subsidy changes, and food and housing costs kept inflation high in the early 2020s.
2020s.driver: Exchange rate, food, housing, subsidy changes
2020s.outcome: The 2024 endpoint shows one of the steepest cumulative price climbs in the supported set.
